summary: DHH announces that 37signals is leaving the cloud after over a decade of running services on AWS and Google Cloud. He argues that renting computers is a bad deal for medium-sized companies with stable, predictable growth, noting that Basecamp and HEY are paying enormous premiums for elasticity they don't need. He dismantles the marketing narratives around cloud simplicity, pointing out that operations teams don't shrink just because you move to the cloud. Beyond cost, he frames the decision as a matter of internet decentralization, arguing it's tragic that a handful of mega corporations now control the infrastructure of a system designed to be distributed.
key_points: Cloud computing only makes economic sense at two extremes: very simple/low-traffic apps, or highly irregular/unpredictable loads — neither applies to 37signals today
HEY pays over $500K/year just for database and search services from Amazon, a premium DHH considers absurd given how much hardware that could buy outright
The promise that cloud simplicity would reduce operations staff never materialized — running major services in the cloud is not actually simpler overall
AWS prints nearly 30% profit margins renting servers, but the industry frames it as futuristic 'computing on demand' rather than the mundane reality of renting computers
The concentration of the internet on a few mega-corporations' infrastructure is a threat to the decentralized architecture the internet was built on
money_quotes: Renting computers is (mostly) a bad deal for medium-sized companies like ours with stable growth.
It's like paying a quarter of your house's value for earthquake insurance when you don't live anywhere near a fault line.
Do you know how many insanely beefy servers you could purchase on a budget of half a million dollars per year?
Anyone who thinks running a major service like HEY or Basecamp in the cloud is "simple" has clearly never tried.
The cloud is sold as computing on demand, which sounds futuristic and cool, and very much not like something as mundane as "renting computers", even though that's mostly what it is.
It strikes me as downright tragic that this decentralized wonder of the world is now largely operating on computers owned by a handful of mega corporations.

Don't let the entrenched cloud interests dazzle you into believing that running your own setup is too complicated. Everyone and their dog did it to get the internet off the ground, and it's only gotten easier since.
key_insight: For companies with stable, predictable workloads, the cloud is an expensive rental arrangement dressed up in futuristic marketing — and its dominance threatens the decentralized nature of the internet itself.
